text.title: Risk Assessment Agentic Patterns
text.summary: Multi-pass agent pipeline, enumeration and pre-mortem prompt patterns, gotchas, and best practices for automated risk assessment.

section.title: Multi-Pass Pipeline

rule.statement: Drive risk assessment as a 4-pass pipeline: (1) researcher agent enumerates candidate risks; (2) red-team agent runs pre-mortem; (3) scorer agent applies prob x impact with citations; (4) human owner reviews and assigns mitigations.
rule.rationale: A single-pass enumeration produces a list anchored on the agent's prior knowledge. A separate red-team agent with a different system prompt (assume failure) surfaces black-swan and tail risks that the enumeration agent's optimism heuristic drops.

rule.statement: Never let an agent autonomously execute an "Avoid" decision. Avoid means killing a feature or market — always require a human-in-the-loop gate before executing.
rule.rationale: Avoiding a risk terminates a product direction. An agent making this call autonomously can invalidate weeks of work. The gate must be explicit and logged.

example.code:
Enumeration pass prompt: You are a risk analyst. Given the product brief in <brief>, enumerate risks across 5 categories: market, product, team, financial, operational. For each risk: one-sentence statement, evidence (URL or doc ref), probability (H/M/L), impact (H/M/L). Minimum 3 risks per category. Cite sources inline.
Pre-mortem pass prompt: It is 12 months from today. The launch in <brief> failed. Write a 500-word post-mortem explaining what went wrong. Then convert each failure mode into a risk row. Be specific: no "lack of focus" — name the channel, the hire, the assumption.
example.note: Enumeration and pre-mortem use different system prompts and different agent instances. Merging them into one pass produces a list biased toward known risks.

section.reference: Bash script that sorts register by score and lints missing fields

rule.statement: Require evidence per row. Reject any risk without a URL, conversation reference, or internal metric.
rule.rationale: Risks without evidence are vibes-based register entries. A URL or document reference forces the agent to ground the risk in observable reality, not inference.

rule.statement: Track mitigation cost vs. expected loss. If a mitigation costs more than prob x impact in dollars, accept the risk instead.
rule.rationale: Teams systematically over-spend on low-EV risks (compliance theater) and under-spend on high-EV risks (channel concentration). The cost comparison provides a decision rule.

rule.statement: Diff the risk register across monthly reviews. The delta — new risks, retired risks, score changes — is the actual signal.
rule.rationale: The absolute register is a point-in-time snapshot. The diff reveals whether identified risks are materializing, being mitigated, or being silently ignored.

antipattern.description: LLMs assign H/M/L confidently with no basis. Ungrounded probability scores inflate register quality perception.
antipattern.reason: Force the agent to cite a source or comparable case for any score. Reject rows where the probability is not grounded in evidence or a base rate.

antipattern.description: Risk register drift on re-runs: re-running the enumeration agent yields a different ID set every time.
antipattern.reason: Use stable hashes (slug of risk statement) instead of sequential IDs, or have the agent diff-merge instead of regenerate, to preserve register continuity across runs.

antipattern.description: Pre-mortems need divergent perspectives; a solo agent run collapses to one viewpoint.
antipattern.reason: Use 3+ agents with different system prompts (skeptic, customer, competitor) and consolidate. A single-agent pre-mortem produces a list skewed by the single prompt's optimism level.

antipattern.description: Risk statements often contain customer names, vendor terms, or unlaunched plans — confidentiality leak risk before external sharing.
antipattern.reason: Run a scrubber agent before sharing the register externally or piping it into a third-party SaaS.
